messages.content:
We would like to invite you for a HK tax briefing tomorrow (i.e. June 28, 2023) at 11:00am HK time. We have created a Zoom meeting and the dial in details are as below.

check grammar

We would like to invite you to a tax briefing for Hong Kong tomorrow (June 28, 2023) at 11:00am HK time. We have organized a Zoom meeting, and the dial-in details are provided below. Please let us know if you have any questions or concerns.

Grammar check: 
- The grammar in the sentence is correct.

Thank you for your reply. 

We would like to invite you for a HK tax briefing on June 29, 2023 at 11:00am HK time. We have created a Zoom meeting and the dial in details are as below.


check please

Your message looks grammatically correct to me. However, you may consider adding more information such as:

- Who is the intended audience of the tax briefing?
- What is the purpose of the tax briefing?
- Will there be any specific topics covered during the briefing?
- How long is the tax briefing expected to last?

Providing additional context could help your message become more informative and effective.

After confirmation with the HK Inland Revenue Department, they needs more time to prepare the file number for him as this is his first year of arriving in HK. Thus, his 2022/23 Hong Kong Individual Tax Return has not issued to client and IRD can not confirm the final tax demanded. However, based on our calculation, the estimsted tax of 2022/23 is no tax liability for the year.

check sentences

Here's a corrected version of your message:

"After confirming with the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department, we have learned that more time is needed to prepare a file number for your client, as this is his first year of arrival in Hong Kong. As a result, the 2022/23 Hong Kong Individual Tax Return has not yet been issued to him, and the IRD cannot confirm the final tax demanded. However, based on our calculations, we estimate that there will be no tax liability for the year." 

I made some minor changes to improve overall clarity and readability of your message.

Please note we have checked with the HK Inland Revenue Department (“IRD”) and have been informed that Marcin’s 2022/23 HK Indiviudal Tax Return is not issued as of today. As such, the final tax demanded is not available yet. 

For your information, please note that we have alrady prepared the 2022/23 tax computation in respect of Marcin Lenart. Based on the estimated calculation, the estimsted tax of 2022/23 is NIL for the year. However, please note that the tax computation will be subject to the IRD’s review and discretion.


check please

Here's a corrected version of your message:

"Please be advised that we have checked with the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department ("IRD") and have been informed that Marcin's 2022/23 HK Individual Tax Return has not been issued as of today. As such, the final tax demanded is not available yet.

We would also like to inform you that we have already prepared the 2022/23 tax computation for Marcin Lenart. Based on our estimated calculation, we anticipate that there will be no tax liability for the year. However, please note that the tax computation will be subject to the IRD's review and discretion." 

I made a few minor changes for clarity and to make the message more concise.
so my messages are not good enough ?

I apologize if my suggestions made it seem like your messages were not good enough. That was not my intention. 

Your messages were grammatically correct, but I made some suggestions to improve clarity and make the message more concise. However, if you feel that your original messages effectively convey the necessary information, you can certainly use them as is. 

In any case, please don't hesitate to ask if you have any further questions or concerns.
but you think yours is better?

As an AI language model, I cannot make judgments or opinions. However, I made suggestions to improve the clarity and conciseness of your messages based on common writing practices. Ultimately, you should use the message that best conveys the information and tone you want to communicate to your recipient.

Our client was based outside Hong Kong and did not visit Hong Kong nor render any services in Hong Kong during the Year of Assessment 2022/2023. Therefore, our client should be entitled to claim full exemption under Section 8(1A)(b)(ii) of the Inland Revenue Ordinance in respect of the income derived from his employment with HSBC Global Services (HK) Limited during the Year of Assessment 2022/2023.

check sentence

Your sentence looks grammatically correct to me. However, you may consider adding more information such as:

- Why is your client entitled to claim full exemption under Section 8(1A)(b)(ii) of the Inland Revenue Ordinance?
- What was the client's work arrangement with HSBC Global Services (HK) Limited during the Year of Assessment 2022/2023?
- Is there any additional information the recipient should know about your client's tax situation?

Providing more context could help your message become more informative and effective.
need to use "nor" or "or"?

In this context, you should use "nor" since it is expressing a negative connection between two phrases. 

When you use "nor" after a negative statement, it indicates that the second item does not apply either. Here is an example: "I do not like pizza, nor do I like pasta." This means that the speaker does not like either pizza or pasta.

On the other hand, "or" is commonly used to present a choice or alternative. For example, "Would you like tea or coffee?"

2022/23 NOA issued without taking into account of full exemption (no services rendered).  IRDQ issued for 2022/23 and yet to reply.  

check sentence

Your sentence looks grammatically correct to me. However, you may consider adding more information such as:

- What is a NOA (Notice of Assessment)? 
- Why was the full exemption not taken into account when the NOA was issued? 
- What is IRDQ and why is it relevant to your client's tax situation?
- Are there any next steps your client needs to take in response to the IRDQ?

Providing more context and detail can help your message become more informative and effective.

We understand that you have a eTAX account, you may also login your eTAX account and check if you will be able to file your 2022/23 Return via eTAX. 

check

Your sentence looks grammatically correct to me. However, you may consider making a few minor edits for clarity:

"We understand that you have an eTAX account. You may login to your eTAX account and check if you are eligible to file your 2022/23 return via eTAX." 

I removed "will be able to" and replaced it with "are eligible to," as this phrasing suggests that the eTAX account is available for use and that the user must confirm their eligibility.

As mentioned that HK tax year runs from 1 April to 31 March the following year. Since you started your STA in Hong Kong on March 22, 2023 and only 9 days before the end of tax year 2022/2023, you are still required by the HK Inland Revenue Department (“IRD”) to file your 2022/23 HK individual tax filings. Please note you have the tax filing obligation for the year 2022/23 while the IRD will review your case to determine whether a HK Individual Tax Return will be issued to you for completion. 

check grammar

Your sentence looks grammatically correct to me. Here is the sentence with minor edits for clarity:

"As mentioned, the HK tax year runs from April 1 to March 31 of the following year. Since you started your stay in Hong Kong on March 22, 2023, just nine days before the end of the 2022/23 tax year, you are still required by the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department (“IRD”) to file your HK individual tax filings for that year. Please note that you have the tax filing obligation for 2022/23, and the IRD will review your case to determine whether a HK Individual Tax Return will be issued to you for completion." 

The changes I made were primarily for clarity and readability, as well as making the language slightly more concise.

We have just received your 2022/23 Employer’s Return (Form IR56B) and will prepare your 2022/23 Individual Tax Return accordingly.

In order for us to officially update the Inland Revenue Department ("IRD") on our tax representative status so that we can work with them directly on your behalf, we would appreciate it if you could complete, sign and return us the attached Tax Representative Appointment Letter by email.

check grammar

Your sentence looks grammatically correct to me. Here is the sentence with minor edits for clarity:

"We have received your 2022/23 Employer’s Return (Form IR56B) and will prepare your 2022/23 Individual Tax Return accordingly.

In order for us to officially update the Inland Revenue Department ("IRD") on our tax representative status so that we can work with them directly on your behalf, we would appreciate it if you could complete, sign, and return the attached Tax Representative Appointment Letter to us by email." 

The changes I made were primarily for clarity and making the language slightly more concise.

We have just received your 2022/23 Employer’s Return (Form IR56B) from your employer and will arrange to prepare your 2022/23 Individual Tax Return accordingly.

Meanwhile, we note that you have yet to provide the signed Tax Representative Appointment Letter further to our previous communication. We would appreciate it if you could complete, sign, and return the attached Tax Representative Appointment Letter to us by email, we will then submit the letter to the IRD accordingly.

check please

Your message looks grammatically correct to me. However, you may consider making a few minor edits for clarity:

"We have received your 2022/23 Employer’s Return (Form IR56B) from your employer and will arrange to prepare your 2022/23 Individual Tax Return accordingly.

We also note that you have yet to provide the signed Tax Representative Appointment Letter following our previous communication. We would appreciate it if you could complete, sign, and return the attached Tax Representative Appointment Letter to us via email, so that we can submit the letter to the IRD accordingly. 

Thank you in advance for your attention to this matter." 

I made a few changes for clarity and to streamline the language slightly.

As mentioned, the HK tax year runs from April 1 to March 31 of the following year. Since you started your STA to Hong Kong on March 22, 2023, just 9 days before the end of the 2022/23 tax year, you are still required by the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department (“IRD”) to file your HK individual tax filings for that year. Please note that you have the tax filing obligation for 2022/23, and the IRD will review your case to determine whether a 2022/23 HK Individual Tax Return will be issued to you for completion. 

We understand that your payroll is still maintained in your home country, however, as you are performing services in HK, your employment income will be treated as arising in and derived from Hong Kong (regardless where your employment income is paid). As such, your employment income will be subject to HK salaries tax.

please check

Your sentence looks grammatically correct to me. Here is the sentence with minor edits for clarity:

"As mentioned, the Hong Kong tax year runs from April 1 to March 31 of the following year. Since you began your short-term assignment in Hong Kong on March 22, 2023, just nine days before the end of the 2022/23 tax year, you are still required by the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department (“IRD”) to file your HK individual tax filings for that year. Please note that you have the tax filing obligation for 2022/23, and the IRD will review your case to determine whether a 2022/23 HK Individual Tax Return will be issued to you for completion.

We understand that your payroll is still maintained in your home country. However, as you are performing services in Hong Kong, your employment income will be treated as arising in and derived from Hong Kong, regardless of where your employment income is paid. As such, your employment income will be subject to HK salaries tax." 

The changes I made were primarily for clarity and readability.

Please note that you have the tax filing obligation for 2022/23, and the IRD will review your case to determine whether a 2022/23 HK Individual Tax Return will be issued to you for completion.

However, as you are performing services in HK, your employment income will be treated as arising in and derived from Hong Kong, regardless of where your employment income is paid. Your message looks grammatically correct to me. Here is the message with minor edits for clarity:

"As mentioned, the Hong Kong tax year runs from April 1 to March 31 of the following year. Since you began your short-term assignment in Hong Kong on March 22, 2023, just nine days before the end of the 2022/23 tax year, you are still required by the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department (“IRD”) to file your HK individual tax filings for that year. Please note that you have the tax filing obligation for 2022/23, and the IRD will review your case to determine whether a 2022/23 HK Individual Tax Return will be issued to you for completion.
